paragraphs.text: A composite measure of seismic exposure, infrastructure fragility, and retrofit coverage across 20 metropolitan areas on the Pacific tectonic boundary. Updated every ninety days from twelve open data sources.
Fig. 1 · National datasets normalized to share of pre-1990 building stock. Indonesia and Türkiye anchor the unretrofitted tail; Japan and Taiwan lead certified retrofits.
The composite index weights four normalized sub-indicators against a 100-point scale calibrated on the 2011 Tōhoku and 2010 Maule events as reference benchmarks.
The increase is concentrated in cities with downgraded infrastructure-age estimates following the 2024 OECD audit. No new seismic events drove the shift.
Fourteen of twenty ranked metros now exceed the panel's elevated-exposure threshold. Quito and Anchorage crossed this quarter for the first time.
Indonesia's certified retrofit rate, the lowest in the panel. Indonesia, Nepal, and the Philippines together account for 8.6M of the 34M aggregate at-risk population.

Median Index Score
        66.4
        Median composite risk across all twenty ranked Pacific Rim metros — a 4.2-point increase over Q2 2024, driven primarily by infrastructure-age recalibration.

Cities Above Threshold
        14
        Fourteen of twenty cities now score above the 60-point "elevated structural exposure" threshold established by the 2023 panel.

Aggregate Population
        34.0M
        Thirty-four million residents live within the high-liquefaction or surface-rupture zones identified by national geological surveys.

visibleText: Meridian Fault Dispatch Seismic Risk · Infrastructure · Displacement Vol. VII · No. 3 · Q3 2024 Front Investigation Data Desk Field Notes Archive Data Desk · Quarterly Index Pacific Rim Risk Index — Q3 2024 A composite measure of seismic exposure, infrastructure fragility, and retrofit coverage across 20 metropolitan areas on the Pacific tectonic boundary. Updated every ninety days from twelve open data sources. RELEASED 14 OCT 2024 NEXT UPDATE 15 JAN 2025 METHODOLOGY v3.2 LICENSE CC BY-SA 4.0 20 Cities ranked 6 Country groups 12 Source datasets 87.3 Top index score Ranked Composite Risk · 20 Cities Scores 0–100 · higher = greater risk # City Country Pop. at Risk Infra Age Retrofit % Index 1 Tokyo Japan 4.82M 38 yr 61% 87.3 2 Manila Philippines 3.91M 44 yr 12% 84.9 3 Jakarta Indonesia 3.20M 41 yr 9% 82.1 4 Lima Peru 2.74M 47 yr 7% 80.6 5 Istanbul Türkiye 2.58M 36 yr 22% 78.4 6 Osaka Japan 2.11M 35 yr 58% 74.2 7 Quito Ecuador 1.88M 39 yr 14% 72.8 8 Taipei Taiwan 1.74M 31 yr 68% 71.5 9 Santiago Chile 1.62M 29 yr 54% 69.9 10 Anchorage USA 0.42M 42 yr 31% 68.4 11 Kathmandu Nepal 1.51M 48 yr 6% 67.7 12 Portland USA 1.08M 37 yr 28% 65.1 13 Seattle USA 1.21M 34 yr 33% 63.8 14 Christchurch New Zealand 0.38M 25 yr 71% 61.2 15 Mexico City Mexico 2.94M 33 yr 26% 59.6 16 San Francisco USA 0.87M 32 yr 47% 57.4 17 Vancouver Canada 0.74M 30 yr 41% 54.9 18 Wellington New Zealand 0.21M 27 yr 66% 52.3 19 Los Angeles USA 1.95M 29 yr 52% 49.8 20 Hobart Australia 0.09M 26 yr 38% 31.4 Median Index Score 66.4 Median composite risk across all twenty ranked Pacific Rim metros — a 4.2-point increase over Q2 2024, driven primarily by infrastructure-age recalibration. Cities Above Threshold 14 Fourteen of twenty cities now score above the 60-point "elevated structural exposure" threshold established by the 2023 panel. Aggregate Population 34.0M Thirty-four million residents live within the high-liquefaction or surface-rupture zones identified by national geological surveys. Retrofit Coverage vs. Building Stock · 6 Countries % of pre-1990 stock 100 75 50 25 0 Japan Taiwan Chile USA-WC Türkiye Indonesia % of stock Country group · pre-1990 building stock distribution Retrofit certified Partial / pending Unretrofitted Fig. 1 · National datasets normalized to share of pre-1990 building stock. Indonesia and Türkiye anchor the unretrofitted tail; Japan and Taiwan lead certified retrofits. Methodology The composite index weights four normalized sub-indicators against a 100-point scale calibrated on the 2011 Tōhoku and 2010 Maule events as reference benchmarks. Indicator weights Population in mapped surface-rupture and liquefaction zones (35%); median infrastructure age weighted by occupancy (25%); certified retrofit coverage of pre-1990 stock (25%); hospital and emergency-route density (15%). Data normalization Each indicator is z-scored across the panel, clipped at ±2.5σ, and rescaled to a 0–100 range. Country reporting lag is corrected by a Kalman-filter imputation step trained on the 2018–2023 panel. Geographic boundaries Metropolitan footprints follow OECD Functional Urban Area definitions where available; otherwise national statistical-agency boundaries are used. Footprint vintage is 2022 for all cities. Confidence intervals Bootstrapped 1,000-iteration intervals are computed per city. Median 95% CI width across the panel is ±3.4 points; widest is Kathmandu (±7.1) due to sparse retrofit reporting. Revision policy Scores are revised quarterly. Major methodology changes are versioned (current: v3.2, Oct 2024) and applied retroactively across the full 2018–2024 panel to preserve comparability. Key Findings +4.2 Median index rose since Q2. The increase is concentrated in cities with downgraded infrastructure-age estimates following the 2024 OECD audit. No new seismic events drove the shift. 14 / 20 Cities cross the 60-point line. Fourteen of twenty ranked metros now exceed the panel's elevated-exposure threshold. Quito and Anchorage crossed this quarter for the first time. 9% Indonesia's certified retrofit rate, the lowest in the panel. Indonesia, Nepal, and the Philippines together account for 8.6M of the 34M aggregate at-risk population. Retrofit gap remains structural. Data Sources & Citations USGS Earthquake Hazards Program — National Seismic Hazard Maps, 2023 revision (data.usgs.gov/earthquakes). Meridian Fault Dispatch — Country audit interviews, May–Aug 2024, on-record sourcing. Taiwan Architecture & Building Research Institute — Retrofit Census, Q1 2024. Servicio Geológico Mexicano — Risk overlay grids, 2023. Nepal Department of Urban Development — Reconstruction Authority status report, June 2024.
